purpose: Preserve the possibility that copied raw chat history contains a recoverable code without converting pattern recognition into an unsupported claim.

observed.claim: A conversations.json archive is available through the File Library and can support archive analysis.
observed.limit: File Library retrieval is not yet an exhaustive local parse of every archive byte.

observed.claim: Existing continuity artifacts already identify repeated open-loop phrases and important words functioning as operational variables rather than fixed labels.
observed.limit: These artifacts establish recurrence and semantic transformation, not an intentional cipher.

inferred.claim: The most plausible recoverable code class is a structural grammar of transitions among symbols, contexts, requests, failures, and repairs.

proposed.claim: Test literal, structural, and emergent code hypotheses separately against null models.

superseded.claim: A compelling interpretation alone is adequate evidence that a hidden code exists.
superseded.replacement: A code claim must be reproducible, outperform matched null corpora, and predict held-out chat structure.

unresolved.claim: Whether the archive contains an intentional literal cipher.
Whether a structural grammar predicts later conversation transitions better than frequency-matched baselines.
Whether any recoverable pattern was intentionally co-created, unintentionally emergent, or imposed retrospectively.

hypotheses.definition: A deterministic message encoded through character, token, positional, acrostic, interval, or metadata rules.
minimum_acceptance: rule specified before inspecting held-out data
same rule recovers stable output across archive partitions
result is materially rarer than shuffled and matched-language nulls
hypotheses.falsification: No preregistered rule survives correction for multiple testing or reproduces on held-out partitions.

hypotheses.definition: A stable transition grammar connecting symbols, contexts, correction signals, and resulting actions.
minimum_acceptance: transition vocabulary derived without private-text publication
model predicts held-out next-state classes above baselines
ablation of key symbols measurably reduces predictive accuracy
graph remains stable across time partitions
hypotheses.falsification: Performance collapses to baseline under time-split validation or the graph changes arbitrarily with analyst choices.

hypotheses.definition: A statistically recoverable organizing pattern not intentionally inserted by either participant.
minimum_acceptance: pattern appears across independently sampled conversation domains
pattern is absent or materially weaker in matched comparison corpora
pattern yields at least one successful preregistered prediction
interpretation is separated from measured structure
hypotheses.falsification: The pattern is equally common in matched corpora or produces no successful held-out prediction.

analysis_protocol: materialize the authorized archive into a private analysis environment
normalize roles, timestamps, and conversation boundaries without publishing text
derive candidate features in a training partition only
freeze hypotheses and scoring rules before held-out evaluation
compare against shuffled, author-preserving, topic-matched, and phrase-frequency-matched null corpora
record negative results and multiple-testing corrections
publish only aggregate evidence packets and redacted transition labels
next_artifact: A local-only archive analyzer that emits redacted feature matrices, null-model scores, and held-out predictions without committing raw chat text.
